documents.title: Requirements Gathering
documents.summary: Requirements gathering turns vague business intent into explicit functional, operational, and constraint-based decisions a delivery team can act on.
key_points: Separate business outcomes, user needs, constraints, and assumptions early so architecture discussions stay grounded.
Non-functional requirements are often the real design drivers because they reshape cost, security, and resiliency choices.
Capture open questions explicitly so later design debates do not silently reopen discovery work.
examples: Translate 'must scale globally' into traffic profile, latency targets, and data residency constraints.
Identify compliance, integration, and support boundaries before choosing a platform.
pitfalls: Letting stakeholder opinions stand in for measurable requirements.
Treating non-functional requirements as minor details that can be filled in later.
avoid_when: Do not move into detailed solution design while core assumptions and constraints are still implicit.

documents.title: Stakeholder Management
documents.summary: Stakeholder management keeps architecture decisions aligned with the people who own outcomes, budgets, operations, and delivery risk.
key_points: Different stakeholders optimize for different things: speed, compliance, cost, customer impact, or run support.
Architecture work stalls when decision-makers, influencers, and approvers are not separated clearly.
Good stakeholder management reduces late surprises more than it improves slide quality.
examples: Map who signs off on security, cost, and operational support before solution review.
Frame the same architecture choice differently for finance, delivery, and platform teams.
pitfalls: Assuming the loudest stakeholder is the final decision-maker.
Presenting technical detail without clarifying business impact.
avoid_when: Avoid treating stakeholder alignment as a presentation task instead of a decision-management task.

documents.title: Tradeoff Analysis
documents.summary: Tradeoff analysis compares credible options against the criteria that actually matter in the business and delivery context.
key_points: Tradeoffs only become useful when criteria such as cost, speed, resiliency, skill fit, and lock-in are explicit.
A downside that seems small in build phase can dominate run cost and support burden later.
Rejecting an option is just as valuable as selecting one if the reason is clear.
examples: Compare serverless and containers using latency, cost shape, and team skill fit.
Evaluate commercial products against in-house builds by including support and integration cost.
pitfalls: Comparing options without a shared decision frame.
Optimizing only for initial delivery speed and ignoring long-term operating cost.
avoid_when: Do not present every option as equally viable when several should be eliminated early.

documents.title: Migration Strategy
documents.summary: Migration strategy defines how to move from current state to future state while controlling business disruption, technical risk, and operational complexity.
key_points: Phased migration reduces blast radius but creates temporary coexistence and reconciliation complexity.
Big-bang migration simplifies architecture shape but concentrates risk into the cutover window.
Rollback, data migration, and support readiness usually matter more than the target-state diagram.
examples: Run parallel systems until data reconciliation confidence is high enough for cutover.
Carve out a bounded capability behind a stable API rather than moving everything at once.
pitfalls: Underestimating data quality issues and hidden integration dependencies.
Planning cutover without clear rollback criteria.
avoid_when: Avoid large simultaneous migrations when business continuity is sensitive and rollback is weak.

documents.title: Buy vs Build
documents.summary: Buy-vs-build decisions should weigh business differentiation against delivery speed, integration effort, and long-term operating cost.
key_points: Buy commodity capabilities unless control, differentiation, or constraints make that a poor fit.
Build when the capability is strategically unique or vendor fit introduces unacceptable dependency cost.
License price is rarely the full cost; governance, adaptation, and run ownership are usually larger.
examples: Buy identity or observability tooling to accelerate delivery of non-differentiating capabilities.
Build custom workflow orchestration when domain rules create real competitive advantage.
pitfalls: Choosing a product from demos without validating integration and exit costs.
Building internally because the team prefers it, not because the business benefits.
avoid_when: Avoid custom builds for undifferentiated capabilities with mature market options.

documents.title: Reference Architecture
documents.summary: Reference architecture gives teams a reusable pattern baseline so common solution decisions do not start from scratch every time.
key_points: A reference architecture should guide decisions, not freeze every implementation detail.
It helps consistency across teams by clarifying approved patterns for identity, networking, data, and observability.
Good reference architectures evolve with delivery feedback instead of becoming static compliance art.
examples: Publish a standard integration pattern for APIs, eventing, and secrets management.
Use reference architectures to speed early solution framing for repeated workloads.
pitfalls: Making reference architecture so rigid that it blocks legitimate context-specific design.
Letting patterns age without reflecting what teams actually deploy.
avoid_when: Avoid presenting reference patterns as mandatory if the context clearly needs an exception.

documents.title: Security by Design
documents.summary: Security by design means integrating identity, data protection, segmentation, and threat thinking into the architecture from the start.
key_points: Security controls are easier to implement and cheaper to maintain when they are part of the initial design shape.
Identity, secrets, logging, and network boundaries are architectural concerns, not post-build hardening tasks.
The goal is risk reduction aligned to the business context, not generic control accumulation.
examples: Define trust boundaries, encryption, and audit needs during solution framing.
Design least-privilege service access and operational logging before implementation.
pitfalls: Treating security review as a gate after the design is already fixed.
Adding controls that do not map to actual business or threat priorities.
avoid_when: Do not assume managed services eliminate the need for security architecture decisions.

documents.title: Cost Optimization
documents.summary: Cost optimization is the discipline of matching architecture choices to actual value, load shape, and operational efficiency.
key_points: The cheapest design on day one is often not the cheapest design under real traffic and support conditions.
Fixed versus variable cost shape matters as much as total estimated spend.
Good cost optimization protects business outcomes without silently undercutting resilience or delivery speed.
examples: Compare always-on clusters with serverless options based on steady versus bursty demand.
Use cost telemetry to decide whether convenience features are worth their operational savings.
pitfalls: Optimizing spend by removing reliability controls the business actually depends on.
Ignoring observability and support cost when comparing platform options.
avoid_when: Avoid treating infrastructure unit price as the only architecture cost metric.

documents.title: Operational Readiness
documents.summary: Operational readiness ensures a solution can be observed, supported, recovered, and governed before it faces production pressure.
key_points: A solution is not ready if alerting, runbooks, ownership, and recovery procedures are undefined.
Readiness should include support handoff, capacity assumptions, access review, and incident response expectations.
Managed services reduce toil but do not remove the need for run design.
examples: Define dashboards, on-call ownership, rollback steps, and access boundaries before launch.
Validate backup and restore by testing the restore path, not just configuring backups.
pitfalls: Treating go-live as a code-complete milestone instead of a run-readiness milestone.
Shipping without clear ownership for incidents and change control.
avoid_when: Do not assume platform teams will fill operational gaps that the solution never defined.

documents.title: Failure Mode Thinking
documents.summary: Failure mode thinking asks how the solution breaks under stress, dependency loss, bad data, human error, and partial outages before those conditions occur in production.
key_points: Architects should identify likely failure paths and decide which ones deserve prevention, mitigation, or graceful degradation.
Many incidents come from dependency behavior, operational mistakes, or untested assumptions rather than core business logic.
Thinking about failure early usually improves observability and recovery design too.
examples: Model what happens if an identity provider, queue, or downstream API becomes slow or unavailable.
Test whether retries, timeouts, and circuit breakers protect the system or amplify failure.
pitfalls: Optimizing only for the happy path and leaving degradation behavior undefined.
Adding retries everywhere and accidentally increasing load during failure.
avoid_when: Do not rely on high-availability claims from vendors without understanding actual failure boundaries.
